title: Comparative advantage and the gains from trade
description: Practice what you have learned about calculating absolute advantage and comparative advantage, and how to use comparative advantage to determine who should specialize in the production of one good or the other.
topic_path: Comparative advantage and the gains from trade
Comparative advantage and the gains from trade

assessment_items.question_content:
Alex and Ella are completing a project for class together that requires them to complete worksheets and write essays, and they are trying to decide how to best use their time to divide and conquer this assignment. 

The input table below gives the time that it takes Ella and Alex to complete their homework assignments in hours.

 | Time to complete Worksheets | Time to complete Essays
- | - |
Ella | $1$ hour | $2$ hours
Alex  | $1$ hour | $3$ hours 

**Who has absolute advantage in each good?**
[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: Alex has absolute advantage in essays and Ella has absolute advantage in worksheets
Ella has absolute advantage in essays and Alex has absolute advantage in worksheets
Both have absolute advantage in both goods.
Ella has absolute advantage in essays, but neither has absolute advantage in worksheets
Alex has absolute advantage in essays, but neither has absolute advantage in worksheets

assessment_items.question_content:
Two agents with identical resources can each make the same two goods:  baseball trophies and racing medals.

**Which of the following best captures the idea of absolute advantage for these two agents?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: An agent is able to produce more of a good or service than another economic agent given the same resources.
One economic agent has a lower opportunity cost of one good, and another economic agent has a lower opportunity cost of a different good.
Both agents have identical opportunity costs.
One agent has higher opportunity costs of one good, and the other agent has higher opportunity costs of the other good.
One agent is able to produce less of a good or service than another economic agent.

assessment_items.question_content:
Martha and Dave like to make identical tacos and cakes. The time it takes Martha and Dave to make tacos and cakes is given in the table below.

|Martha | Dave
-|-|-
Time to make a taco | 10 minutes | 15 minutes
Time to make a cake | 20 minutes | 30 minutes

**Which person has an absolute advantage in the production of tacos, and which person has an absolute advantage in the production of cakes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Martha has an absolute advantage in producing both goods.
Dave has an absolute advantage in producing both goods.
Neither Dave nor Martha has an absolute advantage at producing either good.
Both Martha and Dave have an absolute advantage in producing both goods.
Martha has an absolute advantage in tacos; Dave has an absolute advantage in cakes.

assessment_items.question_content:
Max and Eli like to build apps and coach soccer. The time it takes for Max and Eli to build apps and plan soccer drills for their team is given in the table below.

|Max | Eli
-|-
Time to build an app | 20 hours | 15 hours
Time to write a soccer drill | 5 hours | 9 hours




**Which person has an absolute advantage in building apps, and which person has an absolute advantage in planning soccer drills?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Eli has an absolute advantage in building apps; Max has an absolute advantage in planning soccer drills.
Eli has an absolute advantage in producing both goods.
Neither person has an absolute advantage in producing either good.
Both have an absolute advantage in producing both goods.
Max has an absolute advantage in building apps; nobody has an absolute advantage in planning soccer drills.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jessica can produce $2$ boats in a day or $100$ umbrellas in a day. Paul can produce $3$ boats in a day or $120$ umbrellas in a day.

This means that for every boat that Jessica produces, her opportunity cost is $50$ umbrellas. For every boat Paul produces, his opportunity cost is $40$ umbrellas.

**Which of the following best illustrates the idea of comparative advantage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Jessica produces fewer boats in a day than Paul, so she has comparative advantage in producing boats.
Paul is relatively better at producing boats because he gives up fewer umbrellas per boat.
Paul is better at producing boats because he can produce more boats in a day.
There is no comparative advantage in this case because Paul can produce more goods in a day.
Jessica produces fewer umbrellas in a day than Paul, so she has comparative advantage in producing boats.

assessment_items.question_content:
The PPC below shows how many airplanes and pizzas Country A and Country B can produce with a certain number of workers.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which country has comparative advantage in pizza?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The country that has comparative advantage in pizza is the country that gives up fewer airplanes. We can figure this out by comparing the slopes of the PPCs.
Country A gives up one airplane for each one million pizzas, and Country B gives up 1.5 airplanes per one million pizzas. Therefore, Country A has comparative advantage in producing pizzas.

options.alt: A coordinate grid plots two straight downward-sloping lines labeled A and B. The horizontal axis is labeled Pizzas (millions), and the vertical axis is labeled Airplanes. Line A starts at (0, 8) and ends at (8, 0). Line B starts at (0, 6) and ends at (4, 0). Line A is above and to the right of Line B.
options.caption: The production possibilities of two countries

choices.content: Country A
Country B
Both Country A and Country B
Neither Country A nor Country B
We cannot determine this

assessment_items.question_content:
Alex and Henry are completing group work for class. The input table below gives the time that it takes Henry and Alex to complete their homework assignments in hours.

 | Time to complete Worksheets | Time to complete Essays
- | - |
Henry | $1$ hour | $2$ hours
Alex  | $1$ hour | $3$ hours 

**Who has comparative advantage in producing essays?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Alex
Henry
Neither Henry nor Alex
Both Henry and Alex
We cannot determine who has comparative advantage

assessment_items.question_content:
Martha and Dave like to make tacos and cakes. The time it takes for Martha and Dave to make tacos and cakes is given in the table below.

|Martha | Dave
-|-
Time to make a taco | 10 minutes | 15 minutes
Time to make a cake | 20 minutes | 30 minutes


**Which person has a comparative advantage in the production of tacos, and which person has a comparative advantage in the production of cakes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Neither person has comparative advantage in producing either good.
Martha has a comparative advantage in producing both goods.
Dave has a comparative advantage in producing both goods.
Both have a comparative advantage in producing both goods.
They both have a comparative advantage in producing tacos; neither person has a comparative advantage in producing cakes.

assessment_items.question_content:
Eric and Melanie like to build apps and coach soccer. The time it takes Eric and Melanie to build apps and plan soccer drills for their team is given in the table below.

| Eric | Melanie
-|-
Time to build an app | 20 hours | 20 hours
Time to write a soccer drill | 5 hours | 10 hours

**Which person has a comparative advantage in the production of apps, and which person has a comparative advantage in the production of soccer drills?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Melanie has a comparative advantage in building apps; Eric has a comparative advantage in creating soccer drills.
Melanie has a comparative advantage in creating soccer drills; Eric has a comparative advantage in building apps.
Neither person has a comparative advantage at producing either good.
Both people have a comparative advantage in producing both goods.
Eric has a comparative advantage in building apps; nobody has a comparative advantage in planning soccer drills.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements about specialization and trade is true?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: A country should specialize in and export the good for which it has absolute advantage.
A country should specialize in and import the good for which it has comparative advantage.
A country should specialize in and import the good for which it has absolute advantage.
A country should specialize in and export the good for which they have comparative advantage.
Both countries should specialize and trade in the same good if their opportunity costs are equal.

assessment_items.question_content:
The PPC below shows how many airplanes and pizzas Country A and Country B can produce with a certain number of workers.

[[☃ image 1]]

**If the two countries were to specialize and trade with one another, which country would import airplanes?**

[[☃ radio 3]]

Line A starts at (0, 8) and ends at (8, 0). Line B starts at (0, 6) and ends at (4, 0). Line A is above and to the right of Line B.

choices.content: Country A
Country B
Neither
Both
We can't determine this

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements is true about specialization and trade?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: A country should specialize in and export the good for which it has absolute advantage.
A country should specialize in and import the good for which it has comparative advantage.
A country should specialize in and import the good for which it has absolute advantage.
A country should specialize in and export the good for which they have comparative advantage.
A country should specialize in and export a good for which they have both comparative and absolute advantage

assessment_items.question_content:
Joya can make an apple pie in $40$ minutes and she can prepare a turkey in $60$ minutes.

Chris needs $30$ minutes to make a pie but requires $50$ minutes to prepare a turkey.

**Based on this information, who should focus on making turkey for Thanksgiving dinner?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Chris
Joya
Neither
Both
We cannot tell

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following situations would allow agents to gain from specialization and trade with one another?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: The terms of trade are between the two agents' opportunity costs.
The agents have the same opportunity costs.
One agent has absolute advantage in both goods.
One agent has comparative advantage in both goods.
Either agent has absolute advantage in either of the goods.

assessment_items.question_content:
Country A and Country B are potential trading partners who can produce boats and umbrellas, as illustrated in the PPCs below.

[[☃ image 1]]

Suppose the terms of trade are one boat for one umbrella.

**Who would want to sell boats based on these terms of trade, and who would want to buy boats based on these terms of trade?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A coordinate grid with grid lines at each unit plots two downward-sloping lines labeled A and B. The horizontal axis is labeled Umbrellas and ranges from 0 to 11 in increments of 1. The vertical axis is labeled Boats and ranges from 0 to 11 in increments of 1. Line A starts at (0, 10) and ends at (5, 0). Line B starts at (0, 10) and ends at (8, 0). Line A is steeper and lies to the left of Line B.

choices.content: Neither country would be willing to buy or sell boats given these terms of trade because the prices of these goods are not given in money.
Country A would want to buy boats, and Country B would want to sell boats.
Country A would want to sell boats, and Country B would want to buy boats.
Both countries would want to sell given these terms of trade, so  there will be no exchange.
Neither country would be willing to sell given these terms of trade because neither country has comparative advantage in either good.

assessment_items.question_content:
Country A and Country B are potential trading partners that can produce boats and umbrellas, as illustrated in the PPCs below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following exchange prices for boats would result in gains from trade for these two countries?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A coordinate grid plots two downward-sloping straight lines on a graph. The vertical axis is labeled Boats and is marked from 0 to 11 with grid lines at every 1 unit. The horizontal axis is labeled Umbrellas and is marked from 0 to 11 with grid lines at every 1 unit. One line extends from (0, 10) to (5, 0) and is labeled A. The second line extends from (0, 10) to (8, 0) and is labeled B. Line A lies to the left of line B. Both lines represent linear trade-offs between the quantities of Boats and Umbrellas.
options.caption: PPC for Country A and Country B

choices.content: $\dfrac{1}{4}$ umbrella per boat
$\dfrac{1}{3}$ umbrella per boat
$\dfrac{3}{5}$ umbrella per boat
One umbrella per boat
2.5 umbrellas per boat

assessment_items.question_content:
Alex and Henry are completing group work for class. The input table below gives the time that it takes Henry and Alex to complete their homework assignments in hours.

 | Time to complete Worksheets | Time to complete Essays
- | - |
Henry | $1$ hour | $2$ hours
Alex  | $1$ hour | $3$ hours 

**Assuming Alex and Henry decide to specialize and trade essays and worksheets with one another, what would be a terms of trade or exchange price that would satisfy both of them?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
In the time that it takes Henry to do an essay, he can also do $2$ worksheets, so his opportunity cost of an essay is $2$ worksheets.

In the time that it takes Alex to do an essay he can also do $3$ worksheets, so his opportunity cost of an essay is $3$ worksheets
An acceptable trading price will lie between the two opportunity costs.

An essay should trade for between $2$ and $3$ worksheets, such as $1.5$ worksheets for each essay. Alex would "sell" essays in exchange for worksheets, and Henry would "sell" worksheets in exchange for essays.

choices.content: Any price where an essay costs less than $2$ worksheets
Any price where an essay costs between $2$ and $3$ worksheets
Any price where an essay costs more than $3$ worksheets
We cannot determine a mutually agreeable trading price
Any trading price is possible
